title: Learning Controllable Representations
speaker: Richard Zemel
affiliation: University of Toronto; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: As deep learning systems become more prevalent in real-world applications it is essential to allow users to exert more control over the system. Exerting some structure over the learned representations enables users to manipulate, interpret, and even obfuscate the representations, and may also improve out-of-distribution generalization.

proof_stages.label: Motivation for structured transferable representations
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames the goal as learning multipurpose neural representations whose factors of variation are separated, noting transfer, interpretability, fairness, and the limits of fully unsupervised disentanglement before announcing a supervised program.

proof_stages.label: From classifier information loss to bijective preservation
proof_stages.summary: A standard classifier is described as retaining class identity while discarding nuisance variation, motivating a replacement viewpoint in which an invertible map preserves all input information while still exposing class-relevant information.

proof_stages.label: Invertible representation with supervised and nuisance coordinates
proof_stages.summary: Normalizing flows and reversible networks are reviewed, then the final representation z is split into z_s, the class-logit coordinates used by a softmax, and z_n, the remaining coordinates intended to carry nuisance information.

proof_stages.label: Hybrid preimages reveal excessive invariance
proof_stages.summary: By combining z_s from one image with z_n from another and inverting the network, the speaker shows that the classifier output can remain fixed while the visible image changes drastically, defining a complementary adversarial vulnerability called excessive invariance.

proof_stages.label: Information-theoretic diagnosis of cross entropy
proof_stages.summary: The mutual-information decomposition shows that ordinary cross entropy only encourages high I(y;z_s) and gives no incentive to remove residual class information from z_n, so true disentanglement requires information separation or independence.

proof_stages.label: Independence cross-entropy via an adversarial discriminator
proof_stages.summary: The proposed ICE objective combines the usual class cross entropy on z_s with a discriminator trained to predict y from z_n while the representation is trained to make that discriminator fail, thereby reducing class information in z_n.

proof_stages.label: Position relative to prior information-separation methods
proof_stages.summary: The approach is contrasted with the information bottleneck, fair representation learning, entropy-estimation methods, MMD, variational autoencoders, style/content models, and ICA, emphasizing the role of exact invertibility and absence of a reconstruction-loss tradeoff.

proof_stages.label: Empirical validation and extensions
proof_stages.summary: Experiments on MNIST hybrids and spurious-cue distribution shifts show improved separation and robustness under ICE, and the speaker sketches extensions to multiple supervised factors and domain adaptation.

examples.name: Decapitated image classifier reused for detection/tracking
examples.note: An image-classification network with its last layer removed is used to make concrete the idea that intermediate representations transfer to object detection, tracking, and other tasks, but it does not supply the later method.
examples.engagement_reason: The decapitated classifier is used as an introductory illustration of transfer to detection and tracking, with no construction or analysis carried out on it.

examples.name: Telltale-pixel MNIST distribution shift
examples.note: A one-hot pixel perfectly correlated with digit class at training time is removed at test time to demonstrate that forcing all class information into the supervised code improves robustness to a spurious cue.
examples.engagement_reason: The telltale-pixel MNIST shift is concretely constructed, the test perturbation is specified, and training versus adversarial error rates are compared.
